title: Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting Overview
subtitle: Plane-based writing, authored space, representation, and writing-systems questions
summary: Overview entry for the writing-systems series of the Sutton SignWriting platform.

slides.title: What This Series Argues
slides.summary: This series asks what kind of writing SignWriting is and how spatial authorship should be described.

image_prompts.concrete: Writing-systems overview slide showing point, line, plane, authored space, signbox composition, handwriting, and machine encoding
image_prompts.abstract: A written sign opening into typology, authored space, and representation

bullets: Machine encoding must preserve human writing
slides.speech: Writing and Representation asks what kind of writing SignWriting is. It develops the claims that writing is not only linear, that SignWriting uses authored space, and that machine encoding should preserve rather than replace human signed writing.
slides.notes: Make this feel like theory with practical consequences.

slides.title: Terms Map
slides.summary: The series distinguishes plane-based organization, authored space, iconicity, convention, handwriting, encoding, and kinematography.

image_prompts.concrete: Concept map slide linking plane-based writing, authored space, iconicity, convention, handwriting, machine encoding, and kinematography
image_prompts.abstract: A writing-systems vocabulary map around a composed sign

bullets: Encoding preserves written structure for machines
slides.speech: The series builds a vocabulary. Plane-based writing describes organization. Authored space describes composition. Iconicity and convention explain legibility. Handwriting shows material practice. Machine encoding preserves authored written structure. Kinematography names a broader representational question.
slides.notes: This slide turns the series into a usable map.

slides.title: Reading Order
slides.summary: Readers can move from typology into authored space, legibility, handwriting, machine encoding, kinematography, and comparison with other scripts.

image_prompts.concrete: Writing and Representation reading path slide through Shape of Writing, Plane-Based Writing, Perspective, Iconicity, Handwriting, Machine Encoding, Kinematography, and Other Scripts
image_prompts.abstract: A theoretical sequence moving from typology to authored writing and encoding

bullets: Start with plane-based writing and authored space
Then read human writing and machine encoding
Use kinematography and script comparison for broader theory
slides.speech: The reading order moves from typology into authored space, then into legibility and handwriting, then into machine encoding. Kinematography and the comparison with other sign-language scripts widen the theoretical frame.
slides.notes: Keep this as orientation, not a full argument.

slides.title: Claim Boundaries
slides.summary: The series extends writing-systems vocabulary without making typology, evidence, handwriting, or encoding do the same job.

image_prompts.concrete: Writing-systems boundary slide separating typology, authored space, legibility, handwriting, machine encoding, evidence, and formal text models into related but distinct areas
image_prompts.abstract: A composed written sign surrounded by clear conceptual boundaries

bullets: Keep evidence and encoding in their own layers
Use theory to clarify later series
slides.speech: The series is strongest when its claims stay bounded. It extends writing-systems vocabulary without making one term do every job. Typology, authored space, legibility, handwriting, machine encoding, evidence, and formal text models are related, but they remain different layers of the platform.
slides.notes: Close the overview by protecting the theory from overextension. This slide should make the series feel precise rather than speculative.
